subsystem: Flatpak D-Bus portal service — the org.freedesktop.portal.Flatpak 'Spawn' method handler (handle_spawn). This is the privileged, host-side (unsandboxed) daemon method that a sandboxed flatpak application calls over the session bus to ask the host to launch a new flatpak instance ('flatpak run ...') on its behalf. It parses caller-supplied argv, working directory, file-descriptor map, environment variables, flags, and a sandbox-options dictionary, assembles a 'flatpak run' command line and environment, and executes it on the host via g_spawn_async_with_pipes.
provenance: Identified from the D-Bus generated types and naming: the PortalFlatpak *object / GDBusMethodInvocation signature, the FLATPAK_METADATA_GROUP_* / FLATPAK_METADATA_KEY_* and FLATPAK_SPAWN_FLAGS_* / FLATPAK_SPAWN_SANDBOX_FLAGS_* constants, portal_flatpak_complete_spawn, GLnxTmpfile/glnx_steal_fd (libglnx), and flatpak_instance_new_for_id. This is flatpak-portal.c handle_spawn from the flatpak project. The sample banner (primevul_c_validation_0305_vuln) and the CWE-74 filename mark it as an injection-class sample.
trust_boundary: The D-Bus caller is a sandboxed flatpak application. Every 'arg_*' method parameter is fully attacker-controlled and hostile: arg_cwd_path, arg_argv (the command and its arguments), arg_fds (a {uh} fd map), arg_envs (a {ss} dictionary of environment variables), arg_flags, and arg_options (a dictionary carrying sandbox-expose lists, sandbox-flags, and sandbox-expose-fd handles). The app_info GKeyFile (line 56) is the caller's instance metadata resolved by the portal from the peer's credentials — it is trusted-by-the-portal but is a distinct, lower-risk source than the direct method arguments. The handler runs OUTSIDE the sandbox with the user's full host privileges, so anything the caller can steer into the host process launch crosses the sandbox boundary.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the server-side handler for the org.freedesktop.portal.Flatpak.Spawn D-Bus method. 'object'/'invocation' are the generated GDBus context. 'fd_list' is the out-of-band GUnixFDList carrying the real file descriptors the caller passed. 'arg_cwd_path' is the requested working directory; 'arg_argv' is the NULL-terminated command vector to run; 'arg_fds' is a GVariant 'a{uh}' mapping destination fd numbers to fd-list handles; 'arg_envs' is a GVariant 'a{ss}' of environment variable name/value pairs; 'arg_flags' is a bitmask of FLATPAK_SPAWN_FLAGS_*; 'arg_options' is an 'a{sv}' options dictionary. Every arg_* value originates from the sandboxed caller and is therefore untrusted.

The local state for the launch. 'child_setup_data' is passed to the post-fork child-setup callback (it carries the fd map, a tty flag, and env/instance-id fds). 'fds'/'fds_len' will point at the raw descriptors from the GUnixFDList. 'fd_map' is a heap array of FdMapEntry {to, from, final} describing how caller handles are remapped in the child. 'env' is the environment vector that will be handed to the spawned process. 'flatpak_argv' is the GPtrArray accumulating the 'flatpak run ...' command line. The remaining g_autofree/g_auto(GStrv) locals hold strings and string-lists pulled from app_info metadata (app id, branch, arch, commits, shares, sockets, devices, extra_args).

The two setup fds are initialised to -1 (invalid). If a GUnixFDList was supplied, g_unix_fd_list_peek_fds borrows the raw descriptor array and records its length in fds_len — these are the descriptors the fd map will index. app_info is fetched from data previously attached to the invocation by the portal's authentication step; it is the caller's instance metadata (application name, runtime, instance path, etc.). Both app_info and app_id are asserted non-NULL, so a caller whose metadata is missing would abort the daemon rather than proceed (a robustness concern but not the focus here).

Initial argument validation. An empty app id means the caller is not really a flatpak and is rejected. An empty cwd string is normalised to NULL (meaning 'inherit'). A NULL or empty argv is rejected as 'No command given'. arg_flags is masked against FLATPAK_SPAWN_FLAGS_ALL and any unknown bit is rejected — good hygiene that constrains the flag namespace but says nothing about the semantic power of the accepted flags.

The runtime reference is read from app_info metadata and, if absent, the call is rejected. runtime_ref is then split on '/' into runtime_parts. A well-formed flatpak runtime ref looks like 'runtime/org.foo.Platform/x86_64/1.0', so parts [1] and [3] are expected to be the name and version. The rest of this block (lines 103-129) pulls branch, instance_path, arch, extra_args, app_commit, runtime_commit, shares, sockets, devices and the devel boolean from app_info — these drive the eventual 'flatpak run' arguments and the sandbox context.

The caller-supplied options dictionary is decoded: sandbox_expose / sandbox_expose_ro are string arrays of paths to expose into a sandbox, sandbox_flags is a bitmask, and sandbox_expose_fd / _ro are arrays of fd handles. sandbox_flags is masked against FLATPAK_SPAWN_SANDBOX_FLAGS_ALL and unknown bits are rejected (line 137). Lines 144-152 additionally reject sandbox-expose requests when the caller has no instance_path (nothing to expose from).

Each requested expose path (read/write and read-only) is validated through is_valid_expose before use, and an invalid one aborts the call. This is a deliberate validation gate on caller-controlled path strings that will later become '--filesystem' style arguments, preventing arbitrary path exposure syntax.

The fd map is built. n_fds is the number of {uh} entries in arg_fds (only counted if a real fd list was supplied). For each entry, dest_fd (the 'u' destination number, wanted by the caller) and handle (the 'h' index into the fd list) are read. The handle is bounds-checked against fds_len and rejected if out of range or negative, so fds[handle] is a safe read. The resulting real descriptor (handle_fd) is recorded as the 'from', dest_fd as the 'to'/'final'. Lines 210-221 additionally mark a controlling tty when a std fd is a tty and track max_fd for later reallocation.

A second pass resolves collisions where a requested 'to' descriptor number coincides with a not-yet-consumed 'from' descriptor of a later entry; on conflict the 'to' is bumped to a fresh number above max_fd so the child-side dup sequence does not clobber a source fd. This is bookkeeping for the child-setup dup2 logic and is not directly attacker-steerable beyond the handle values already validated.

This constructs the environment that will be handed to the spawned host process. If CLEAR_ENV is set the base is empty, otherwise it is the portal daemon's own environment. Then EVERY caller-supplied name/value pair from arg_envs is applied via g_environ_setenv with overwrite=TRUE. The caller can set, add, or override ANY environment variable — there is no name allow/deny list and no exclusion of security-sensitive variables (PATH, LD_PRELOAD, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, GIO_*, etc.). This 'env' vector is later passed straight to g_spawn_async_with_pipes (line 568).

The command line begins with the program name 'flatpak' (a bare name, not an absolute path — this matters, see below) and the subcommand 'run'. The 'sandboxed' flag selects between two branches: if sandboxed, socket/share/device options are translated from sandbox_flags and the app's declared sockets/shares/devices (lines 277-308); if not sandboxed, the app's extra_args are appended, and any '--env=NAME=VALUE' extra-arg is validated and packed into env_string as NUL-separated entries (lines 311-336) rather than placed on the command line.

The NUL-separated env_string (from app_info extra_args) is written into a sealed memfd/tmpfile and passed to the child as '--env-fd=N'. This is the out-of-band, side-channel-free way of delivering environment data. Note this path is fed by trusted app_info metadata, NOT by the untrusted arg_envs — the arg_envs values instead go directly into the process 'env' vector at lines 260-267.

PID-namespace sharing is gated behind a runtime support check (line 365 requires FLATPAK_SPAWN_SUPPORT_FLAGS_EXPOSE_PIDS, i.e. non-setuid bwrap) and the caller's own instance id, adding --parent-pid / --parent-share-pids / --parent-expose-pids. The NOTIFY_START branch (lines 400-425) creates a pipe, kicks off an async read of at most INSTANCE_ID_BUFFER_SIZE-1 bytes into instance_id_read_data->buffer, and passes the write end as --instance-id-fd. The bounded read length correctly reserves room for a NUL terminator.

The runtime name/version are pulled from runtime_parts[1] and [3] with no check that g_strsplit produced that many elements. The caller's cwd and the first argv element become '--cwd=' and '--command=' options. Each option is a single, self-contained argv element built with g_strdup_printf, and the vector is later handed to execv (via g_spawn) — there is NO shell, so embedded spaces or shell metacharacters in these strings cannot inject additional arguments or commands into 'flatpak run'.

The app ref (id/arch/branch) and the remaining caller argv elements are appended, then the vector is NULL-terminated. Finally g_spawn_async_with_pipes is invoked with (char **) flatpak_argv->pdata as argv, 'env' as the environment, and flags G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH | G_SPAWN_DO_NOT_REAP_CHILD | G_SPAWN_LEAVE_DESCRIPTORS_OPEN, plus child_setup_func/child_setup_data. On success the pid is recorded in a PidData tracked in client_pid_data_hash and the D-Bus reply is completed. The critical detail is that argv[0] is the bare name 'flatpak' and G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH is set, so the executable is resolved by searching the PATH found in 'env'.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core sandbox-escape primitive (CVE-2021-21261). The sandboxed caller fully controls arg_envs, and every name/value pair is written unfiltered into the 'env' vector that becomes the environment of a HOST process spawned outside the sandbox. There is no allow-list, no deny-list, and no exclusion of security-sensitive variables. Combined with line 568-569 (env passed to g_spawn_async_with_pipes together with G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH and argv[0]=='flatpak'), the caller can set PATH to a directory that is visible on the host filesystem (e.g. under ~/.var/app/<id>/ which is host-accessible) and containing a malicious executable named 'flatpak'; g_spawn then resolves 'flatpak' via that attacker-controlled PATH and executes attacker code on the host with the user's privileges. Sensitive dynamic-linker variables (LD_PRELOAD / LD_LIBRARY_PATH / LD_AUDIT) and GIO/GTK module variables provide equivalent code-injection vectors into the host-side 'flatpak run' process. The invariant the implementation must uphold: caller-supplied environment variables must never be able to alter how, or which, host binary is located and loaded — sensitive variables must be stripped/reset and the launcher resolved by absolute path.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if (!g_spawn_async_with_pipes (NULL,
                                 (char **) flatpak_argv->pdata,
                                 env,
                                 G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH | G_SPAWN_DO_NOT_REAP_CHILD | G_SPAWN_LEAVE_DESCRIPTORS_OPEN,
                                 child_setup_func, &child_setup_data,
                                 &pid,
                                 NULL,
                                 NULL,
                                 NULL,
                                 &error))
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The dangerous half of the pair above. argv[0] is the bare string 'flatpak' (line 270) and G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH is requested, so glib locates the program by scanning the PATH entry of the very 'env' array the attacker just populated (lines 260-267). If PATH is attacker-controlled, the wrong 'flatpak' is executed on the host. Even absent PATH abuse, passing the attacker environment verbatim to a host-privileged launch is a privilege-boundary violation. The safe contract is to launch via an absolute, trusted path (avoiding G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH) AND to sanitise 'env' so no caller variable can influence loader/module resolution.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
g_ptr_array_add (flatpak_argv, g_strdup_printf ("--runtime=%s", runtime_parts[1]));
  g_ptr_array_add (flatpak_argv, g_strdup_printf ("--runtime-version=%s", runtime_parts[3]));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: runtime_parts comes from g_strsplit(runtime_ref, "/", -1) at line 101 with no verification of element count. If the runtime metadata is malformed (fewer than 4 slash-separated components), runtime_parts[1] and especially runtime_parts[3] read past the produced NULL-terminated vector, an out-of-bounds read that either passes a NULL into g_strdup_printf ("(null)") or dereferences beyond the array. runtime_ref derives from app_info (portal-resolved, not the direct method argument) so this is lower severity than the env issue, but the implementation should assert g_strv_length(runtime_parts) >= 4 before indexing.

The 'handle' index into the fd list IS bounds-checked (safe read of fds[handle]). However 'dest_fd' — the destination descriptor number the caller wants in the child — is taken from a 'u' (uint32) into a gint32 and is NOT range-validated. It is stored as fd_map[i].to/.final and later consumed by the child-setup dup logic (not shown). A caller-chosen destination fd number that is negative-after-cast or absurdly large could drive the child_setup_func dup2/close sequence into undefined territory; the implementation must clamp/validate dest_fd against a sane range before the child performs dup2. This is a latent contract on child_setup_func rather than a bug visible in this function.

This branch (non-sandboxed path) demonstrates that the authors KNEW environment data needs careful, out-of-band handling — extra_args '--env=' entries are validated for a non-empty name and a present value and delivered via the sealed --env-fd rather than as command-line args or process env. The contrast makes the omission at lines 260-267 conspicuous: the trusted app_info-sourced env is carefully packaged, while the untrusted arg_envs is dumped straight into the live process environment. If a future edit routed arg_envs into env_string it would still avoid the PATH/loader-search issue only because those go via --env-fd; the arg_envs path does not, which is the defect.

The two g_assert calls abort the entire portal daemon (which serves many clients) if app_info or the application name is missing. In a production build with assertions enabled, a request that reaches this handler without attached app-info metadata is a denial-of-service on the daemon rather than a graceful per-call error. These should be graceful error returns; low severity because reaching here without app-info generally implies a broken authentication path rather than an attacker-reachable state.

summary: handle_spawn is a privileged, host-side Flatpak portal method that launches 'flatpak run' on behalf of a sandboxed caller. Its critical flaw is that caller-supplied environment variables (arg_envs) are written unfiltered into the environment of the spawned host process while the launcher is invoked by bare name under G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH — letting a sandboxed app set PATH/LD_PRELOAD to execute arbitrary code on the host and escape the sandbox (CVE-2021-21261). Secondary issues: unchecked runtime_parts[1]/[3] indexing, an unvalidated caller-chosen dest_fd feeding child-setup dup logic, and daemon-aborting g_assert calls.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: handle_spawn(... arg_cwd_path, arg_argv, arg_fds, arg_envs, arg_flags, arg_options) signature (lines 2-11)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Defines the trust boundary; all arg_* are attacker-controlled inputs to a host-privileged launch.
Unfiltered caller environment applied to a host process — primary sandbox-escape primitive.
Bare 'flatpak' resolved via attacker-controlled PATH; consummates the env-injection escape.
Fixed indices used without checking split element count — out-of-bounds/NULL read on malformed metadata.
Destination fd number is not range-validated before feeding child-setup dup logic.
Assertion aborts the shared daemon instead of a graceful per-call error (DoS/robustness).
Unknown flag bits are rejected; flag namespace is properly constrained.
fd-list index is correctly bounds-checked before fds[handle].
Each caller path string is validated and invalid ones abort the call.
Correctly refuses expose when no instance path exists.
handle >= 0 && handle < fds_len checked before fds[handle].

reviewed_anchors.anchor: --cwd / --command / trailing arg_argv appended as discrete argv elements (lines 540-548)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Passed to execv (no shell) as separate vector elements — no argument/command injection despite attacker content.
Trusted app_info-sourced env is validated and delivered out-of-band; correct handling (contrast with arg_envs).
Read length reserves room for NUL terminator; no overflow of the fixed buffer.
Bookkeeping over already-validated handles; not attacker-steerable beyond validated values.

findings.title: Sandbox escape via unfiltered caller environment + PATH search on host spawn
findings.anchor: g_environ_setenv loop over arg_envs; env passed to g_spawn_async_with_pipes with G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH
findings.bug_class: Environment/command injection leading to sandbox escape (attacker-controlled env + unsafe program lookup)
findings.invariant: Environment variables supplied by a sandboxed caller must never be able to influence how the host launcher binary is located or which shared objects/modules it loads: security-sensitive variables (PATH, LD_PRELOAD, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, LD_AUDIT, GIO_MODULE_DIR, GTK_/GDK_ module vars, etc.) must be stripped or reset, and the launcher must be executed by a trusted absolute path rather than a PATH search.
findings.failure_mode: A sandboxed app calls Spawn with arg_envs containing PATH pointing at a host-visible directory (e.g. under ~/.var/app/<id>/) that holds a malicious executable named 'flatpak'. Because these values are applied unfiltered to 'env' (lines 260-267) and that env is passed to g_spawn_async_with_pipes with argv[0]=='flatpak' and G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH set (lines 270, 566-575), glib resolves 'flatpak' through the attacker's PATH and executes attacker code on the host outside the sandbox with the user's full privileges. LD_PRELOAD / LD_LIBRARY_PATH provide equivalent code injection into the host 'flatpak run' process. Result: full sandbox escape and arbitrary host code execution.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether any allow-list/deny-list filters arg_envs before g_environ_setenv, whether sensitive loader/module variables are reset, and whether the launcher is resolved by absolute path (i.e. whether G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH with argv[0]=='flatpak' can be steered by the caller-provided PATH).

findings.title: Out-of-bounds/NULL read of runtime_parts fixed indices on malformed runtime ref
findings.anchor: runtime_parts[1] / runtime_parts[3] after g_strsplit without element-count check
findings.code:
g_ptr_array_add (flatpak_argv, g_strdup_printf ("--runtime=%s", runtime_parts[1]));
  g_ptr_array_add (flatpak_argv, g_strdup_printf ("--runtime-version=%s", runtime_parts[3]));
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read / NULL pointer read on unchecked array index
findings.invariant: Before indexing runtime_parts[1] and [3], the code must verify g_strsplit produced at least 4 non-NULL components (g_strv_length(runtime_parts) >= 4).
findings.failure_mode: If the runtime reference stored in app_info metadata is malformed (fewer than four '/'-separated parts), runtime_parts[3] reads past the produced NULL-terminated vector — either passing NULL into g_strdup_printf or dereferencing beyond the array end. Because runtime_ref comes from portal-resolved app_info rather than a direct method argument, exploitability is limited, but a corrupted or crafted metadata file yields a crash / undefined read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether runtime_parts length is validated after g_strsplit at line 101 before the indexed uses at lines 529-530.

findings.title: Unvalidated caller-chosen destination fd number feeds child-setup dup logic
findings.anchor: dest_fd read from arg_fds '{uh}', stored in fd_map[i].to/.final
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation on a caller-controlled fd number consumed by dup2/close logic
findings.invariant: The destination fd number (dest_fd) chosen by the caller must be validated against a sane range before child_setup_func performs dup2/close on it, so the caller cannot request an absurd or negative-after-cast target descriptor.
findings.failure_mode: Unlike 'handle', 'dest_fd' is read from a 'u' field into a gint32 and never bounds-checked. It flows into fd_map[i].to/.final and is later used by the post-fork child_setup_func to arrange descriptors via dup2. A pathological dest_fd (e.g. > RLIMIT_NOFILE or a value that collides with critical fds) could cause the child setup to fail or manipulate unintended descriptors. This is a latent contract on the not-shown child_setup_func.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect child_setup_func to confirm dest_fd/fd_map[i].to is validated/clamped before dup2, and that large or reserved fd numbers are rejected.

findings.title: g_assert on app_info/app_id aborts the shared portal daemon
findings.bug_class: Reachable assertion / denial of service via abort
findings.invariant: A single malformed or unexpected request must produce a graceful per-invocation D-Bus error, never abort the long-lived daemon that serves all clients.
findings.failure_mode: If the invocation ever reaches this handler without attached app-info metadata or with a missing application name, g_assert aborts the entire portal process, denying service to every other client. This normally requires a broken authentication path, so severity is low, but assertions on request-derived state should be graceful errors.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether the portal is built with assertions enabled in production and whether any code path can deliver an invocation lacking 'app-info' to this handler.

audit_checklist: Does any filter strip or reset security-sensitive environment variables (PATH, LD_PRELOAD, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, LD_AUDIT, GIO/GTK module vars) from arg_envs before g_environ_setenv at lines 260-267?
Is the launcher executed by a trusted absolute path, or does G_SPAWN_SEARCH_PATH (line 569) with argv[0]=='flatpak' allow the caller-provided PATH to select the binary?
Is g_strv_length(runtime_parts) verified to be >= 4 before indexing runtime_parts[1] and [3] at lines 529-530?
In child_setup_func, is the caller-chosen dest_fd / fd_map[i].to validated/clamped before dup2, and are large/reserved fd numbers rejected?
Are the g_assert calls on app_info/app_id (lines 57, 62) reachable with attacker-influenced state, and should they be graceful error returns?
Do the CLEAR_ENV vs inherit branches (lines 252-258) leave any daemon-internal secrets in the child environment when CLEAR_ENV is not set?
Are sandbox_expose_fd handle_fd paths (lines 462-472, 499-509) constrained so a caller cannot expose arbitrary host paths outside its instance?
